enclosed_documents: Forensic Causation Report with annotated photography
summary_paragraphs: Please find enclosed the complete forensic documentation package for the property at 74 Crestmont Rd, Binghamton, NY. Our independent inspection has documented storm-related damage to the Roofing, Gutters.

The enclosed reports include annotated photography of all identified damage, an Xactimate-format replacement cost estimate, and a forensic causation analysis establishing the relationship between documented weather events and observed damage.
Please do not hesitate to contact us should you require any additional information or wish to schedule a joint inspection.

code_violations.requirement: Asphalt shingles shall be installed in accordance with this section AND the manufacturer's installation instructions — adopts manufacturer spec as code-enforceable requirement
code_violations.status: Required — manufacturer spec controls fastener count, exposure, starter, drip edge, and warranty-critical details

code_violations.requirement: Drip edge required at eaves and rake edges of shingle roofs; min 2-inch overlap at joints, min 1/4-inch projection below sheathing
Underlayment shall be applied in accordance with manufacturer's installation instructions — ASTM D226 / D4869 / D6757 labeling required
Factory starter strip course required along eaves and rakes with pre-applied sealant strip at roof edge; field-cut shingles do not qualify as starter (no sealant = wind-uplift failure)
code_violations.status: Required — included in scope per manufacturer wind-warranty spec

code_violations.requirement: Ice barrier required in areas where the average daily temperature in January is ≤25°F or where ice damming has a history; min 2 feet inside exterior wall line (Climate Zones 5A+ including OH/NY)
Flashings shall be installed at wall and roof intersections, wherever there is a change in roof slope or direction, and around roof openings

conclusion_paragraphs: Based on our forensic analysis of 47 documented findings, the property at 74 Crestmont Rd, Binghamton, NY sustained confirmed storm damage from the July 03, 2025 hail event. Chalk circle testing across multiple roof slopes identified widespread hail impacts characterized by circular depressions, granule displacement, and mat-level bruising — consistent with functional damage that compromises the shingle system's weatherproofing integrity. The rear slope test square alone documented 30 confirmed hail strikes, far exceeding the industry-recognized threshold of 8 impacts per test square that warrants full slope replacement. The consistent strike pattern across front, rear, and adjacent slopes confirms this was not isolated or cosmetic damage but a systemic hail event affecting the entire roof system.

The confirmed functional damage to the 3-Tab 25-year composition shingle roof system and associated components requires full system replacement rather than spot repair. The existing 3-tab product exhibits advanced granule erosion exposing the underlying asphalt mat, a pre-existing condition that increased vulnerability to hail penetration and now renders the damaged shingles non-repairable. Critically, the installed 3-tab shingle profile has been discontinued by manufacturers, and current metric-cut architectural products cannot match the existing exposure dimensions — making piecemeal repair technically infeasible without creating mismatched coursing that violates manufacturer installation requirements and compromises weather resistance. Under the RCNYS and the underlying IRC, replacement materials must provide equivalent or superior weather protection, which necessitates a complete re-roof rather than partial overlay of incompatible products.

Based on the documented damage, applicable building codes under the RCNYS, and industry replacement standards, our analysis identifies the need for full replacement of the asphalt shingle roof system across all slopes, including associated ridge vent components and inspection of the gutter system on all elevations for hail impact damage. The forensic evidence — 30 confirmed hail strikes in a single test square, chalk-documented impacts spanning multiple shingle courses and slopes, and mat-level functional damage throughout — supports a complete roof system replacement scope. The gutter guard and gutter system visible at the ridgeline and eaves should be included in the replacement scope pending elevation-by-elevation hail dent assessment.

This forensic determination was conducted in accordance with HAAG Engineering certified hail damage identification protocols, NRCA technical guidelines for asphalt shingle assessment, and applicable provisions of the IRC as adopted under the RCNYS. The chalk circle testing methodology, impact-per-square quantification, and functional damage classification applied throughout this inspection represent the prevailing professional standard for hail damage causation analysis in the roofing forensics industry.
forensic_findings.damage_summary: This residential property exhibits hail damage to the asphalt shingle roof system. Chalk-marked test squares on the front and adjacent slopes document multiple confirmed hail impacts with granule displacement and mat bruising. The 3-tab shingles show widespread granule loss and aging that makes spot repair infeasible — current metric products cannot match the existing exposure. The shingle surface shows dark staining and granule erosion consistent with an aged roof further compromised by the July 03, 2025 hail event. Full roof replacement is warranted due to confirmed functional hail damage across multiple slopes. Close-up photos of asphalt shingle roof surface show pink/red chalk circle markings used by inspector to document hail impact points across multiple test areas. Chalk-marked impacts are visible on shingle granule surfaces confirming systematic hail damage pattern. Shadow of inspector/phone visible in each frame confirms on-site documentation. Multiple slopes tested with consistent hail strike evidence supporting full replacement claim. Pink chalk testing on asphalt shingle roof reveals hail impact damage across multiple test areas. Chalk circles and marks on the rough shingle surface document confirmed hail strikes with granule displacement. The photographer's shadow indicates overhead sun conditions during inspection. Multiple shingle courses show chalk-documented impacts spanning adjacent shingles, confirming a widespread hail damage pattern consistent with the July 03, 2025 storm event. Roof inspection reveals hail damage across multiple slopes of a 3-tab shingle roof. Chalk-marked test squares on the front and rear slopes document hail hit counts, with notations indicating H=30 on one test square. Close-up photos confirm circular hail impacts with granule displacement and bruised mat at marked locations. Pink chalk circles across the roof field mark individual hail strikes, confirming a widespread damage pattern consistent with the July 03, 2025 storm event. The uniform 3-tab shingle system with documented hail density supports full slope replacement rather than spot repair. Photos 21-25 document hail damage to asphalt architectural shingles via chalk-marked test squares and individual impact close-ups. Multiple slopes show confirmed hail hits with green chalk circles marking impact points where granule displacement and mat bruising are evident. The consistent pattern of impacts across multiple shingles confirms a widespread hail event on July 03, 2025, supporting full roof replacement rather than spot repair. Photos 26-30 document chalk-circled hail impacts on dark gray/charcoal architectural laminate shingles across multiple slopes. Green chalk circles mark individual hail strike locations where granule displacement and mat bruising are confirmed. The consistent pattern of circled hits across multiple shingle courses demonstrates a widespread hail event on July 03, 2025. The density of confirmed impacts per test area supports full slope replacement rather than spot repair. Roof inspection reveals hail damage across multiple slopes of this architectural laminate shingle roof. Chalk-marked test squares on the front and rear slopes document multiple hail impacts per test square, confirming storm damage from July 03, 2025. Close-up of shingle granule surface shows green chalk marks circling individual hail impact points with granule displacement. Ridge cap shingles show granule loss consistent with hail impacts. The shingle system shows aging with granule erosion that compounds storm vulnerability, making spot repair infeasible — full replacement is warranted. Photos 36-40 document hail damage to gutters, downspouts, and siding across multiple elevations. Chalk testing on the downspout (photo 36) confirms circular hail dent patterns on soft aluminum. The gutter system shows impact dents and weathering consistent with a hail event. Downspout sections show hail strikes with green algae staining indicating moisture retention at impact points. The asphalt shingle roof shows aging with granule loss and dark staining but remains secondary to the gutter/downspout damage documented here. Full gutter and downspout replacement is warranted due to widespread hail impacts across all visible sections. This two-story colonial with white vinyl siding and asphalt shingle roof shows hail damage across multiple trades. Chalk testing on the aluminum drip edge/gutter apron confirms hail denting to soft metals. Vinyl siding exhibits hail impact marks, and window trim shows deterioration consistent with storm exposure. The property's siding, gutters, and roofing all sustained damage from the July 03, 2025 storm event. Property at #74 is a two-story colonial with architectural laminated shingles and vinyl/aluminum siding. Chalk-marked hail impacts confirmed on roof shingles with test square documentation. Siding shows hail impact marks on multiple elevations. The roof exhibits widespread hail damage requiring full replacement due to random hit pattern across all slopes. Combined hail and wind damage from the July 03, 2025 storm event is documented.

executive_summary: On July 03, 2025, the residential property at 74 Crestmont Rd, Binghamton, NY sustained storm damage from a hail event affecting its 3-Tab 25-year composition shingle roof system. A comprehensive forensic inspection was conducted, producing 50 photographs documenting the roof's condition across multiple slopes. The inspection employed systematic chalk-marked test squares and individual impact analysis to quantify hail strike density and assess the extent of functional damage to the existing shingle system.

Chalk-marked test squares on both front and rear roof slopes confirmed widespread hail impacts with granule displacement and underlying mat bruising at marked strike locations. One documented test square recorded a density of approximately 30 confirmed hail hits (H=30), with consistent strike patterns observed across all tested slopes. Close-up photography verified circular impact signatures characteristic of hail-induced damage, including fractured granule bonds and exposed fiberglass mat, confirming that the damage mechanism is atmospheric impact rather than normal weathering or wear.

Full roof system replacement is warranted on a technical basis. The existing 3-Tab 25-year shingles exhibit an aged profile with granule erosion and dimensional characteristics that cannot be matched by current metric shingle products, rendering spot repair infeasible under RCNYS material-matching and weatherproofing requirements. The confirmed hail damage density across multiple slopes exceeds any reasonable threshold for isolated repair, and the compromised granule-to-mat bond across the roof field has reduced the system's remaining service life and weather resistance below functional performance standards, necessitating full slope replacement to restore the roof to a code-compliant, weather-tight condition.
key_arguments: Multiple hail impacts confirmed via chalk circle testing across front slope shingles — functional damage documented
3-tab shingles show advanced granule loss exposing dark asphalt mat, increasing vulnerability to hail penetration
Chalk test squares with pink/purple markings identify individual hail strike locations with circular depressions
Ridge vent area shows confirmed hail impact with granule displacement circled by inspector chalk
Aged 3-tab product likely discontinued — spot repair infeasible due to exposure mismatch with current metric products
Gutter guard system visible at ridge; gutter system should be inspected for hail dents on all elevations
Pink chalk circles mark confirmed hail impact points across multiple shingle courses and test areas
Granule displacement and circular depressions visible at each chalk-marked location confirm mat-level functional damage
Consistent hail strike pattern across multiple photos indicates widespread damage, not isolated hits
Architectural laminate shingles show impacts spanning course joints — damage pattern supports full slope replacement
Pink chalk testing confirms multiple hail impacts on architectural laminate shingles from July 03, 2025 storm
Chalk circles document individual hail strikes with circular depressions and granule displacement at impact sites
Damage spans multiple shingle courses and adjacent tabs indicating widespread hail coverage across the roof
Inspector shadow and bright sun conditions confirm overhead documentation during field inspection
Rear slope test square documents H=30 hail hits — far exceeds the 8-hit threshold for full slope replacement per industry standards.
Close-up photos 16-17 show confirmed hail strikes with granule displacement and mat bruising at chalk-circled impact points.
Widespread pink chalk circles across the roof field in photo 18 indicate hail damage is not isolated — full slope pattern consistent with July 03, 2025 storm event.
3-tab shingle profile observed — discontinuation of matching products may necessitate full roof replacement for uniform appearance and warranty compliance.
Multiple hail impacts confirmed via green chalk circle methodology across several shingles on multiple courses
Close-up photos show circular depressions with embedded granules — hallmark of forceful hail impact per HAAG standards
Granule displacement at impact sites exposes dark asphalt mat confirming functional damage requiring replacement
Blue roof jack/ladder bracket visible in photos 21-22 indicates active inspection with safety equipment deployed
Widespread hail impact pattern across multiple shingle tabs supports full slope replacement over spot repair
Green chalk circles across photos 26-30 each mark confirmed hail impact sites with visible granule displacement and circular depressions on dark charcoal laminate shingles.
Hail impacts span multiple courses and cross shingle joints, demonstrating widespread storm damage from July 03, 2025 event.
Impact density across all documented areas supports full slope replacement — spot repair is not feasible given the distribution of confirmed hits.
Mat bruising is evident at circled locations where granules are displaced and asphalt substrate is exposed.
Multiple hail impacts confirmed via green chalk circle markings on shingle surfaces — each circle denotes a verified hail strike with granule displacement
Test square on front slope documents high hail hit count (appears 30+ wind/hail notation) exceeding insurance threshold for full replacement
Ridge cap shingles show concentrated granule loss and exposed mat from hail — highest point on roof sustains most severe impacts
Roof-wide granule erosion visible from ridge overview — aged shingles cannot be spot-repaired with current product due to color/profile mismatch
Chalk test square methodology with circled impacts on right side of test area confirms systematic hail pattern across slope
Chalk test on aluminum downspout (photo 36) reveals multiple circular gaps — each gap is a confirmed hail dent per industry chalk test methodology.
Front elevation downspout shows hail dents along its full vertical run from gutter outlet to grade level.
Aluminum gutter trough and face show hail impact dents with associated mildew/oxidation staining at impact points.
Green algae staining on downspout (photo 39) concentrates at hail dent depressions where moisture is trapped — confirms functional damage to protective coating.
Shingle roof shows aging but directional hail impacts on all soft metals confirm storm event on July 03, 2025.
Chalk test on aluminum drip edge/gutter apron reveals multiple circular gaps confirming hail dents from July 03, 2025 storm
Vinyl siding on all elevations exposed to hail impact — marks visible on rear and side elevations
Window trim deterioration on upper-story window indicates storm-accelerated damage
Soft metal components (drip edge, gutters) confirmed damaged via chalk testing methodology
Multiple elevations show consistent hail exposure pattern confirming widespread storm damage
Chalk test on architectural shingles confirms multiple hail impacts with circular depressions and granule displacement — functional damage per HAAG standard.
Property #74 front and rear elevations expose vinyl siding and soft metals to direct hail path from July 03, 2025 storm.
Brick chimney and all roof penetrations subject to hail exposure — further inspection of flashing recommended.
Multiple chalk-circled impacts on shingle test area confirm storm-caused damage pattern, not age-related wear.
Estimated roof age: ~18 years (installed circa 2008) — Shingle Type: Three-Tab shingle

line_items.supplement_argument: Ventilation required per IRC R806 — 1 SF NFVA per 150 SF attic area (1:150 ratio). OC warranty VOID without proper ventilation. Ridge vent must be R&R during re-roofing

code_citation.jurisdiction: Residential Code of New York State

line_items.supplement_argument: Chimney flashing is code-required at all chimney-roof intersections. Full replacement includes step flashing, counter-flashing, and apron. Reusing old chimney flashing on new roofing is a code violation.

code_citation.jurisdiction: Residential Code of New York State
code_citation.requirement: Flashing shall be installed at wall and roof intersections, at gutters, around roof openings, and at penetrations through the roof plane
code_citation.supplement_argument: Pipe jack flashing is code-required at every roof penetration. When roofing is replaced, all pipe boots must be replaced — reusing old pipe boots on new roofing is a code violation.
code_citation.title: Flashing — General (Pipe Penetrations)

line_items.supplement_argument: Pipe jack flashing is code-required at every roof penetration. When roofing is replaced, all pipe boots must be replaced — reusing old pipe boots on new roofing is a code violation.

photo_integrity.stamp: PHOTO INTEGRITY VERIFIED — 100% | 20 photos analyzed, 0 flagged for manipulation indicators | DumbRoof.AI Fraud Detection Engine
photo_integrity.summary: All 20 photos pass integrity analysis. The photo set represents a standard hail damage roof inspection conducted by a professional inspector using chalk-based documentation methodology. Pink chalk circles mark individual hail impact locations on 3-tab asphalt shingles, with close-up photos showing granule displacement consistent with hail strikes. Test square notation (visible in Photos 18-19) indicates approximately 30 hail hits were documented, with slope direction noted. The blue pole with yellow fittings is a standard inspection chalk holder. Shadows, lighting, and perspective are internally consistent across the entire series. The shingle condition, damage patterns, and random distribution of impacts are consistent with legitimate storm damage. No evidence of staged damage (no tool marks, BB gun impacts, or artificial gouging), no photo manipulation (no cloning, editing artifacts, or impossible lighting), and no misrepresentation detected.

noaa.episode_narrative: Multi-day storm window scan: [2025-07-01] A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. Despite earlier showers, the atmosphere still became unstable later in the morning through early afternoon. In addition, high wind shear and steep temperature lapse rates made conditions favorable for severe weather.  Hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter was observed from the Finger Lakes and south into New York's Southern Tier. One of the storms passed over the NWS Binghamton Forecast Office and dropped large hail on vehicles parked in the lot while also knocking down trees behind the building. A quick survey by a NWS Binghamton employee found trees stripped of their leaves and some crop damage in the Binghamton area. Due to the extensive area of hail reports and the size of the hail, the full extent of the damage to buildings, vehicles and crops is unknown and the estimated damage costs are likely underdone due to this.  In addition, the severe storms also had strong winds. There were numerous other reports for downed trees and wires. In a few cases, trees fell on cars, houses, or other buildings and often resulted in damage. | [2025-07-02] A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. Despite earlier showers, the atmosphere still became unstable later in the morning through early afternoon. In addition, high wind shear and steep temperature lapse rates made conditions favorable for severe weather.  Hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter was observed from the Finger Lakes and south into New York's Southern Tier. One of the storms passed over the NWS Binghamton Forecast Office and dropped large hail on vehicles parked in the lot while also knocking down trees behind the building. A quick survey by a NWS Binghamton employee found trees stripped of their leaves and some crop damage in the Binghamton area. Due to the extensive area of hail reports and the size of the hail, the full extent of the damage to buildings, vehicles and crops is unknown and the estimated damage costs are likely underdone due to this.  In addition, the severe storms also had strong winds. There were numerous other reports for downed trees and wires. events.narrative: Estimated 2 inch hail from social media photo.
NEXRAD radar hail signature — Prob: 100.0%, Severe Prob: 100%
Estimated ping pong hail via social media photo.
NEXRAD radar hail signature — Prob: 100.0%, Severe Prob: 100%

There was an exceptional swath of hail damage, approximately 2.5 miles wide and 5 miles long. Many trees were completely stripped of their foliage, including an entire hillside, which bore the brunt of the storm and appeared entirely bare. Widespread hail damage was also reported to vehicles and homes within the affected area. Spotters reported golf ball sized hail. The hail accumulated in some areas making the ground white and roads slippery.

Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees onto a house and garage. Both structures were damaged.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down numerous trees on Upper Front Street.
Strong thunderstorm winds snapped or knocked down large trees.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on Glen Avenue. Damage to property was also reported.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down numerous trees and wires down throughout Broome County and the Binghamton area.
Strong thunderstorm winds snapped more than 50 poles in the Binghamton area.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees and power lines.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down wires over Airport Road.
Strong thunderstorm winds uprooted a pair of trees onto a vehicle. The vehicle suffered heavy damage. A tow trailer was also lifted and moved.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto wires on Bradley Creek Road.
Strong thunderstorm winds damaged the roof on a horse barn.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees in Kirkwood.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on Conklin-Kirkwood Bridge Road.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down more than 10 trees behind a house.
Strong thunderstorm winds uprooted multiple trees along Pollard Hill Road.
Strong thunderstorm winds snapped large trees along Hardy Road.
Strong thunderstorm winds snapped and uprooted around 40 acres of trees in woods off of Route 26 between East Maine and Hardy Roads.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down large trees on Hill Road.

weather.storm_description: Multi-day storm window scan: [2025-07-01] A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. Despite earlier showers, the atmosphere still became unstable later in the morning through early afternoon. In addition, high wind shear and steep temperature lapse rates made conditions favorable for severe weather.  Hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter was observed from the Finger Lakes and south into New York's Southern Tier. One of the storms passed over the NWS Binghamton Forecast Office and dropped large hail on vehicles parked in the lot while also knocking down trees behind the building. A quick survey by a NWS Binghamton employee found trees stripped of their leaves and some crop damage in the Binghamton area. Due to the extensive area of hail reports and the size of the hail, the full extent of the damage to buildings, vehicles and crops is unknown and the estimated damage costs are likely underdone due to this.  In addition, the severe storms also had strong winds. There were numerous other reports for downed trees and wires. In a few cases, trees fell on cars, houses, or other buildings and often resulted in damage. | [2025-07-02] A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. Despite earlier showers, the atmosphere still became unstable later in the morning through early afternoon.
